title: Mutable vs immutable objects

overview.what: An object is mutable if its contents can change after it is created, and immutable if they cannot. Lists, dicts, and sets are mutable: list.append() edits the same object in place. int, float, bool, str, and tuple are immutable: 'changing' a string actually builds a brand-new string and leaves the original untouched. Think of a mutable object as a whiteboard you keep editing, and an immutable object as a printed page — to 'change' it you must print a new page. Because mutable objects are edited in place, a string or tuple can safely be a dict key while a list cannot.
overview.why: Mutability decides whether an operation edits an object in place or returns a new one. Getting this wrong causes two classic bugs: assuming a string method changed the string (it didn't), and two names sharing one mutable object so an edit through one is unexpectedly seen through the other.

why_learning_this: Avoiding shared-state bugs when the same list or dict is referenced from two places in an API handler
Knowing why a tuple or string can be a dict key but a list cannot

common_mistakes.title: Expecting a string to change in place
common_mistakes.explanation: s.upper() returns a NEW string and leaves s unchanged; calling it without reassigning does nothing. Fix: reassign, e.g. s = s.upper().

common_mistakes.title: Sharing one mutable object by accident
common_mistakes.explanation: After b = a where a is a list/dict, a and b are the SAME object, so a.append(...) is visible through b. Fix: copy it (a.copy()) when you need an independent object.

recall_questions.q: What distinguishes a mutable object from an immutable one?
recall_questions.answer: A mutable object's contents can change after creation (list, dict, set); an immutable object's cannot (int, float, bool, str, tuple).

recall_questions.q: When you 'change' a string, what actually happens?
recall_questions.answer: A new string object is created and the original string is left unchanged.

recall_questions.q: Why can a string or tuple be used as a dict key but a list cannot?
recall_questions.answer: Strings and tuples are immutable so they can safely be keys; a list is mutable and cannot be a dict key.

practice_tasks.title: Reassign string to see uppercase
practice_tasks.prompt: The function below attempts to return an uppercase version of the input string, but it prints the original lowercase text instead. Modify the code to correctly return the uppercase version.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
def make_upper(text):
    text.upper()
    return text

print(make_upper('hello'))
practice_tasks.solution:
def make_upper(text):
    text = text.upper()
    return text

print(make_upper('hello'))

code_walkthrough.code:
nums = [1, 2]
nums.append(3)
print(nums)

text = 'hi'
text.upper()
print(text)
code_walkthrough.focus: The list prints [1, 2, 3] (changed in place); the string still prints 'hi' because .upper() built a new string we ignored.

aha_moment.prediction: backup looks like a safe copy of cart. After appending 'mouse' to backup, what does print(cart) show?
aha_moment.why: backup = cart binds a SECOND label to the same list object — it does not copy. So backup.append('mouse') mutates the one list both names point to, and cart sees it too. For an independent copy use cart.copy().

challenge.title: Merge config without mutating
challenge.prompt: Write merge_config(base, override) that returns a NEW dict combining both (override wins) WITHOUT mutating base. Then print the result and base to prove base is unchanged.

understanding_checks.question: Why does `print(y)` output `[1, 2, 3, 4]` instead of `[1, 2, 3]`?
understanding_checks.answer: Because `y = x` binds the variable `y` to the same underlying list object as `x`. The `+=` operator on a list mutates the object in-place, so `y` reflects the change.
understanding_checks.why: Lists are mutable objects. When multiple variables reference the same mutable object, any in-place mutation to that object through one variable will be visible through all other variables referencing it.

understanding_checks.code:
a = 'hello'
b = a
a += ' world'
print(b)
understanding_checks.question: What is the output of this code?
understanding_checks.why: Strings are immutable. `a += ' world'` does not modify the original string 'hello'. Instead, it creates a new string object 'hello world' and reassigns the variable `a` to point to it. The variable `b` still references the original 'hello' string.
